explanation: The natural language requirement indicates that both prop_0 and prop_1 should hold at some relevant point, typically interpreted as 'eventually' in temporal logic unless 'always' is specified. If this is a requirement about the occurrence as part of the self-test, the formula 'eventually (prop_0 AND prop_1)' captures that both must occur, possibly at the same time.

The requirement is that both prop_0 and prop_1 are confirmed by the self-test procedure, meaning both should hold. Since the temporal aspect isn't specified, we interpret it as these properties globally (always) holding.
The requirement states that the self-test should confirm both prop_0 and prop_1. In LTL, this can be encoded by requiring that eventually (F), both prop_0 and prop_1 hold (i.e., prop_0 & prop_1).

The self-test procedure must confirm both prop_0 and prop_1 hold; in LTL, this usually means it must eventually be true that both prop_0 and prop_1 are confirmed. We use the 'eventually' (F) operator to assert that at some future (possibly current) state, both prop_0 and prop_1 hold together.

The requirement states that the self-test procedure must both satisfy 'prop_0' and be calibrated. Assuming 'prop_1' represents the calibration, the LTL expression is simply 'prop_0 & prop_1', since both must hold at the same point in time.
The requirement states that the self-test procedure should satisfy both prop_0 and prop_1. This is interpreted as: globally, prop_0 AND prop_1 hold.
The requirement states that both prop_0 and prop_1 should hold for the self-test procedure. The most straightforward LTL translation is that globally, both propositions must be true.

The statement requires that both 'prop_0' (which may stand for 'self-test procedure has run') and 'prop_1' (which may represent 'is calibrated') must be true together. There is no reference to time, so the simplest LTL formula is just their conjunction.
'Confirm' in this context is interpreted as 'always holds' for both prop_0 and prop_1. So, globally (G), both prop_0 and prop_1 must hold.

The requirement says that the self-test procedure must (shall) perform prop_0 and prop_1. Interpreting 'shall prop_0 and prop_1' as both need to happen eventually, we express that both prop_0 and prop_1 must eventually be true at some point. In LTL, this can be written as 'eventually prop_0 and eventually prop_1' (F prop_0 & F prop_1).
